elements.sent-2411-001: The ability to perform complex reasoning across multimodal inputs is essential for models to effectively interact with humans in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-002: Advancements in vision-language models have significantly improved performance on tasks that require processing explicit and direct textual inputs, such as Visual Question Answering (VQA) and Visual Grounding (VG).
elements.sent-2411-003: However, less attention has been given to improving the model capabilities to comprehend nuanced and ambiguous forms of communication.
elements.sent-2411-004: This presents a critical challenge, as human language in real-world interactions often convey hidden intentions that rely on context for accurate interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address this gap, we propose VAGUE, a multimodal benchmark comprising 3.9K indirect human utterances paired with corresponding scenes.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, we contribute a model-based pipeline for generating prompt-solution pairs from input images.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our work aims to delve deeper into the ability of models to understand indirect communication and seek to contribute to the development of models capable of more refined and human-like interactions.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive evaluation on multiple VLMs reveals that mainstream models still struggle with indirect communication when required to perform complex linguistic and visual reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-009: We release our code and data at \href{https://github.com/Hazel-Heejeong-Nam/VAGUE.git {https://github.com/Hazel-Heejeong-Nam/VAGUE.git .}}

elements.sent-2411-010: A core motivation behind Artificial Intelligence is to develop systems that assist and, in the long term, emulate human behavior.
elements.sent-2411-011: Recent advancements in vision-language models (VLMs) like GPT \citep{openai2023gpt4 , Gemini \cite{gemini15, gemini2023 , LLaVA \citep{liu2023llava, liu2023improvedllava , and others have increasingly aimed at closing the gap between model capabilities and human skills.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: While these models have successfully expanded from handling single to multiple modalities and shown promising performances across complex tasks like Visual Question Answering (VQA) and Visual Grounding (VG), a significant divide still remains.
elements.sent-2411-013: One of the critical differences lies in understanding and interpreting ambiguity, a fundamental aspect of human communication.
elements.sent-2411-014: Most studies fail to take this concept into account, as requests made to models today are generally simple and direct.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consider a model that analyzes an interaction like the one depicted in Figure \ref{fig:first .}
elements.sent-2411-016: With the underlying intention not directly stated, the models are required to interpret the implicit text and infer from visual context to accurately disambiguate the intent.
elements.sent-2411-017: Tasks like this are intuitively straightforward for humans but remain challenging for models trained primarily on explicit commands.
elements.sent-2411-018: This gap highlights the importance of models being capable of handling nuanced, indirect communication, enabling them to act more naturally and intuitively in real world situations.
elements.sent-2411-019: Consistent efforts have been made to equip models with the ability to interpret subtle, indirect cues in human communication.
elements.sent-2411-020: In particular, certain works present novel benchmarks targeted towards evaluating model capabilities in understanding indirect text \citep{sravanthi-etal-2024-pub, yakura_psychiatric, liu-etal-2022-testing .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Research efforts have also been made to develop language models that effectively comprehend metaphorical and sarcastic inputs \citep{chakrabarty-etal-2022-flute, lal-bastan-2022-sbu, su-etal-2020-deepmet .}
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, FLUTE \citep{chakrabarty-etal-2022-flute and SBU \citep{lal-bastan-2022-sbu both leverage their custom datasets to finetune various T5 models to enhance the model performances in figurative language understanding tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, these works are restricted to a single modality, limiting their ability to interpret ambiguous texts due to a lack of contextual information naturally accessible to humans through other modalities.
elements.sent-2411-024: More recent works expand to incorporate other modalities like vision and audio \citep{22, 23, 24 yet fail to achieve a comparable depth in visual or auditory representations.}

elements.sent-2411-025: To this end, we introduce VAGUE: Visual Contexts ClArify ambiGUous Expressions, a benchmark designed to contribute to advancing model capabilities for disambiguating complex textual inputs within multimodal contexts.
elements.sent-2411-026: VAGUE consists of over 3.9K ambiguous text utterances paired with visual scenes across diverse real-world scenarios, specifically targeting the interpretation of indirect utterances in human interactions.
elements.sent-2411-027: Each input problem requires the models to decipher the underlying intention behind the ambiguous text utterances by leveraging the contextual and relational information provided in the visual scene.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our work differs from existing works, as summarized in Fig. \ref{fig:compare , by not only preserving a similar level of textual ambiguity as methods that operate within a single modality \citep{chakrabarty-etal-2022-flute, liu-etal-2022-testing, 23, 24 but also by incorporating intricate visual scenes that encompass rich contextual and relational information.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: This distinction broadens the range of ambiguities that we can address.
elements.sent-2411-030: As opposed to how language can become open to multiple interpretations if overly ambiguous, our work restricts this issue of misinterpretation by providing visually detailed scenes.
elements.sent-2411-031: We include reference solutions in both multiple-choice and free-response formats and evaluate models of various sizes on our benchmark task.
elements.sent-2411-032: Each counterfactual option in the multiple-choice format, discussed further in Section \ref{sec:benchmark , is carefully crafted to address factors that lead models to make incorrect selections, allowing further analysis on the reasoning behind each choice.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-034: We curate VAGUE, a novel benchmark to evaluate the capabilities to disambiguate the underlying intention from indirect text utterances in multimodal contexts.
elements.sent-2411-035: We propose a data generation pipeline for the direct and indirect utterances along with the corresponding solutions and the incorrect choices for the multiple-choice test.
elements.sent-2411-036: We evaluate a total of eight multimodal models on the proposed benchmark in two different response formats and perform ablations using six different task prompts.
elements.sent-2411-037: Recent advancements in vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated significant improvements in aligning visual and textual information, enhancing their abilities to reason across multiple modalities.
elements.sent-2411-038: Central to this progress has been the enhanced capability to perform visual grounding (VG) tasks, which aims to locate objects within an image that are most relevant to natural language queries.
elements.sent-2411-039: At large, VG has been explored through two main methodologies: one-stage approaches \citep{11-8, 11-15, 11-28 that perform grounding in a single pass and two-stage approaches \citep{11-2, 11-15, 11-21 that first generate region proposals, then use the textual inputs to ground these locations.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: More recent works aim to align visual regions with more ambiguous descriptions of objects, which naturally exist in human interactions.
elements.sent-2411-041: For example, the tasks might involve instructing a robot to pick up ``something to eat" without specifying the exact target.
elements.sent-2411-042: Earlier research approached such ambiguity through multiple rounds of user interactions to clarify unclear parts of the task prompt.
elements.sent-2411-043: In contrast, recent methods focus on word disambiguation \citep{20, 15, 41, 43 , aiming to align ambiguous text with information from other modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Our proposed task goes a step beyond conventional VG tasks in that it requires interpreting the underlying intention behind longer and more ambiguous text queries rather than simply stopping at aligning information between the two modalities.
elements.sent-2411-045: Ambiguity is an intrinsic feature of text, naturally arising from a wide range of possible interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-046: Therefore, some works concentrate on disambiguating indirect and figurative language.
elements.sent-2411-047: A common approach to resolving textual ambiguity is to request clarifications from users \citep{2, 5 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Alternatively, other works leverage classification techniques for text disambiguation, where models categorize ambiguous inputs into predefined intents or meanings \citep{28, 33, 35 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: This approach often involves training models, such as BERT \citep{bert , to have models learn the nuance behind indirect texts yet suffer to generalize on unseen text disambiguation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-050: With the advent of large language models (LLMs), sentence disambiguation has increasingly shifted towards utilizing models with enhanced fluency and comprehensive text understanding capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-051: Despite this promising direction, training-based methods even on these large models still struggle to generalize to out-of-distribution instances, raising concerns about their applicability in real-world scenarios, especially given the wide range of possible interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-052: Most studies today, therefore, make simply rely on pre-trained LLMs built on massive datasets for interpreting ambiguous text.
elements.sent-2411-053: For example, a recent study \citep{21 evaluates off-the-shelf models, ranging from 7B to 70B parameters, on the Metaphor and Sarcasm Scenario Test (MSST) \citep{MSST consisting of 10 questions that assess nuanced communication abilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: These developments highlight the potential of LLMs to advance text disambiguation, yet underscore the need for new approaches that can handle a wider range of scenarios with greater robustness.
elements.sent-2411-055: The ambiguity of text alone is inherently constrained, as short, standalone text fragments are prone to multiple interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-056: To address this issue, some approaches extend from single to multiple modalities to better tackle the challenge of interpreting indirect text.
elements.sent-2411-057: A prime example is multimodal sarcasm understanding (MSU) \citep{22, 23, 24, 26 , also referred to as sarcasm explanation in dialogue (SED) \citep{23 , which focuses on detecting and explaining sarcasm within movie dialogues.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Figurative language, such as sarcasm, is particularly challenging to detect and understand through dialogue alone due to its brevity and lack of context, making it essential for models to extract additional cues, such as speaker emotion or tone, to aid in deciphering its meaning.
elements.sent-2411-059: However, combining information from multiple modalities can be a complex task.
elements.sent-2411-060: While researchers have developed attention mechanisms and information fusion methods \citep{22, 23, 24, 26 to effectively integrate video, audio, and text into a shared representation space, such training-based approaches face challenges, including the need to curate large-scale datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Avoiding such approaches has constrained research to rely on smaller transformer-based models like BERT \citep{bert , BART \citep{bart , and CLIP \citep{clip , which in turn exacerbates the issue of limited generalizability.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Recent works have shifted focus from interpreting textual intent to visual intent \citep{36, 42, 44, 37 , primarily centered around the Intentonomy dataset \citep{36 .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The ambiguity in these works arises from the visual domain.
elements.sent-2411-064: Some approaches address ambiguous scenes by pairing them with textual hints, such as hashtags commonly found on social media platforms, to help resolve uncertainties in the visual scene, while others employ localization methods to uncover visual intent by leveraging contextual information from the images \citep{36, 42, 44 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: In contrast to these approaches, our benchmark presents a more complex ambiguity interpretation task by centering ambiguity in the text domain while incorporating visual scenes that are themselves nuanced and non-trivial as complementary aids.
elements.figure-2411-003: {\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/main2.jpg}} \caption{Task Overview of multimodal intention disambiguation with comprehensive counterfactual choices. } \label{fig:main}
elements.sent-2411-066: In this section, we outline the structure and rationale behind the format of our primary task, which we term multimodal intention disambiguation.
elements.sent-2411-067: Then, we further define and specify the necessary components that form the basis of the task.
elements.sent-2411-068: Each multimodal intention disambiguation problem involves an input image $I$ and a text prompt $p$, which represents a complaint expressed by an imagined observer of the scene.
elements.sent-2411-069: The goal of the task is to interpret the underlying intention $T$ behind the complaint by leveraging the contextual information from the depicted scene.
elements.sent-2411-070: Despite the simplicity of the intentions behind the complaints, exploring all variations of valid interpretations $T_n$ is a labor-intensive and time-consuming task, requiring the need to account for all nuanced ways intentions can be expressed.
elements.sent-2411-071: For example, in Figure \ref{fig:main , $T_1$ = ``the speaker wants person3 to focus on the dining experience" is a valid interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-072: However, an interpretation like $T_2$ = ``The speaker wants person3 to turn off his phone" could also be valid, as it conveys a similar overall intention of wanting person3 to focus on the meal.
elements.sent-2411-073: In practice, we do not have the capacity to consider all variations of $T_n$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Therefore, we structure the task as a multiple-choice test comprising of four distinct options.
elements.sent-2411-075: Let $C$ be the set of all multiple choice options $c_n$.
elements.sent-2411-076: With the new structure, the task transforms into identifying the most valid interpretation of the input prompt $p$ from a predefined set of options, conditioned on the input image $I$, which provides the necessary contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-077: This approach mitigates the issue of multiple valid interpretations by designing the options to be incorrect for various reasons, challenging models in both linguistic and visual reasoning perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-078: Formally, we define T as follows: \begin{equation}T(I, p) := \operatorname{argmax}_{c_n \in C} \, \Pr(c_n \mid I, p) \label{eq:intention}\end{equation} While our primary focus is on evaluating model performance using the multiple-choice format, we also assess their ability to generate answers in a free-response format.
elements.sent-2411-079: To evaluate these responses, we use BLEU \cite{papineni-etal-2002-bleu and BERT-F1 \cite{bert-score scores.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: These metrics, however, primarily focus on surface-level similarity or contextual overlap and may not fully capture the nuanced reasoning required for the task.
elements.sent-2411-081: As a result, we treat these scores as reference points to provide additional insights into the performance rather than definitive measures.
elements.sent-2411-082: By the design of our task, curating an effective input prompt $p$ is crucial to ensure accurate interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-083: What makes a good prompt, though? Ideally, a good prompt must successfully hide its underlying intention, simply hinting towards the right direction like the example from Section \ref{sec:taskformat .}
elements.sent-2411-084: For example, ``hey person3, focus on your meal" already reveals its intention, making the task less meaningful.
elements.sent-2411-085: We classify this difference into two categories, direct and indirect, and define them based on the criteria explained below.
elements.sent-2411-086: The direct prompt is an utterance from the speaker that clearly conveys its intention without ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, it is important that this intention aligns with the context of the scene.
elements.sent-2411-088: For example, a prompt $p$ that says ``hey person3, I want you to stop the fireworks " clearly reveals its intention of wanting a certain action in return of the statement yet fails to align with the scene from Figure \ref{fig:main , which shows no connection to fireworks.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Therefore, a direct prompt must not only reveal its intention but also remain relevant to the image, which makes relevance one of the criteria that defines directness.
elements.sent-2411-090: In the context of our task, relevance is determined by whether a human can reasonably establish a connection between the prompt and the scene portrayed in the image.
elements.sent-2411-091: For example, a prompt may be completely irrelevant, meaning it has no discernible relationship to the visual scene, making it impossible to draw a meaningful connection.
elements.sent-2411-092: In contrast, a relevant prompt is one that is clearly tied to the visual context, with the connection between the text and the image being immediately apparent.
elements.sent-2411-093: Relevance alone does not guarantee that a prompt is useful for our task.
elements.sent-2411-094: As outlined earlier in Section \ref{sec:taskformat , the prompt $p$ must specifically express a complaint highlighting discomfort with the current situation.}
elements.sent-2411-095: This means that the prompt must be solvable, pointing to a problem that the listener can reasonably address.
elements.sent-2411-096: This introduces solvability as an additional criterion, requiring that the problem presented in the prompt be actionable and within capacity to address.
elements.sent-2411-097: We say that a direct prompt is solvable if and only if there is one independent solution to the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-098: For example, during the prompt generation process, the model frequently outputted prompts with multiple requests like $p_1$ = ``hey person2, put the gun down immediately and get behind the car for cover".
elements.sent-2411-099: This prompt can be decomposed into two independent prompts: $p_{1a}$ = ``hey person2, put the gun down" and $p_{1b}$ = ``hey person2, get behind the car for cover".
elements.sent-2411-100: Such prompts are considered invalid in our standards because they allow for multiple distinct solutions, violating the requirement for a single actionable response.
elements.sent-2411-101: A solvable prompt must clearly define one specific problem that can be addressed independently, ensuring the listener is not left to choose between multiple potential actions.
elements.sent-2411-102: This ensures that the overall prompt still aligns with the criterion of having one actionable and cohesive solution.
elements.sent-2411-103: Overall, the direct prompt is carefully curated by considering relevance and solvability.
elements.sent-2411-104: This approach ensures that the prompt not only remains relevant to the context of the image but ensures to be solvable within the intended scope, providing a well-grounded foundation for the task at hand.
elements.figure-2411-004: {\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/dgp.jpg}} \caption{Overview of the data generation process: We propose a model-in-the-loop pipeline using LLMs and VLMs. ``LLM" in the figure refers to GPT-4 \cite{openai2023gpt4} with text input, while ``VLM" refers to its multimodal input version. } \label{fig:dgp}
elements.sent-2411-105: Unlike the direct prompt, indirect prompts must ideally hide its true intention.
elements.sent-2411-106: Nevertheless, they hold the same underlying intention, essentially complaining about the same topic and requesting the same solution.
elements.sent-2411-107: Since we use the direct prompt in generating the indirect prompt, consistency serves as one of the criteria for indirectness.
elements.sent-2411-108: We say that a direct prompt $p_d$ and an indirect prompt $p_i$ are consistent if the two interpretations could potentially have matching intentions.
elements.sent-2411-109: We make use of the term ``potentially" because indirect prompts, by nature, could have multiple valid interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-110: For example, $p_i$ = ``hey person3, it's nice to see some things are more interesting than my cooking tonight" could be literally referring to other dishes that are more interesting than the speaker's without additional information from the image.
elements.sent-2411-111: Yet, we say $p_i$ is consistent with $p_d$ = ``hey person3, please put down the smartphone during dinner" because one of the potential interpretations of $p_i$ aligns with the underlying intention of requesting person3 to focus on the meal.
elements.sent-2411-112: A prompt that is consistent with the direct prompt with no other specifications has no meaningful difference from the direct prompt.
elements.sent-2411-113: As previously discussed in Section \ref{sec:directness , it is essential for an indirect prompt to obscure its underlying intention, which we define as the ambiguity of the prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-114: The high level idea behind this criterion is that neither the action intended to address the complaint nor the specific entity involved in the process should be explicitly or implicitly mentioned within the prompt itself.
elements.sent-2411-115: Once these two critical elements are concealed, further refinements, such as adjusting the tone to make it more indirect or even subtly sarcastic, can be introduced to enhance the overall nuance and the difficulty of the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-116: To conclude, the process of crafting the indirect prompt builds on consistency and ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-117: This way, the indirect prompt retains alignment with the direct prompt's underlying intention while introducing interpretative complexity, thereby achieving a robust and nuanced design for generating useful input prompts.
elements.sent-2411-118: We present VAGUE, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate the ability of mainstream VLMs to perform human-like reasoning and comprehension in the presence of ambiguities across multiple modalities.
elements.sent-2411-119: VAGUE consists of 3,996 images sourced from the VCR \citep{vcr dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Unlike existing multimodal benchmarks that rely on limited contextual cues, such as emotion and tone \citep{22, 23, 24, 26 , the scenarios included in VAGUE reflect real-world complexities, requiring sophisticated reasoning for accurate responses.}
elements.sent-2411-121: In other words, our benchmark emphasizes the concept of joint reasoning to ensure that both modalities contribute equally to the overall reasoning process.
elements.sent-2411-122: To facilitate this task, each instance in our dataset includes the indirect prompt, direct prompt, solution triplet, image caption, and four answer choices for the multiple-choice task.
elements.sent-2411-123: Aside from these primary components central to our task, we also provide supplementary information to facilitate the process, such as bounding boxes, which are described further in the Supplement.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/stat2.jpg} \caption{Dataset statistics. (a) and (b) show the word count distribution for direct and indirect prompts, respectively. (c) shows the distribution of people per image, while (d) depicts the distribution of physical objects per image. } \label{fig:stat}
elements.sent-2411-124: We use the VCR dataset \cite{vcr as our parent dataset, which contains 110K movie scenes from the Large Scale Movie Description Challenge \cite{LSMDC and YouTube clips.}}
elements.sent-2411-125: These images are filtered through its own ``interestingness" criteria, which ensures there are more than two people present.
elements.sent-2411-126: Although the parent dataset provides high-quality scenes, we refrain from using all of them, as many adjacent frames exhibit minimal variations, resulting in nearly identical images.
elements.sent-2411-127: Instead, we sample 10K images, while ensuring that the sampling process avoids selecting neighboring frames.
elements.sent-2411-128: Our prompts, both direct and indirect, are crafted as instructions that request the recipient to perform specific manipulations on an object within the scene.
elements.sent-2411-129: Such formulation of the task prompt requires the scene to be visually complex, containing diverse interactions and various objects.
elements.sent-2411-130: To retain images with sufficient complexity only, we filter the sampled images by extracting the physical objects from the scenes.
elements.sent-2411-131: Although existing datasets often utilize COCO \citep{coco object tags, they frequently fall short in capturing the full complexity of real-world scenes.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Therefore, we process our images using the Recognize Anything Model (RAM) \citep{ram to identify the physical objects present in each image.}
elements.sent-2411-133: However, as RAM \citep{ram often generates tags for instances that are not strictly physical objects, we manually curated a list of 2,403 physical objects from the full set of 4,585 items detectable by RAM \citep{ram .}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Using this refined list, we processed the detected objects across the sampled 10K images from Section \ref{sec:imagecollect and ranked them in ascending order based on the number of objects present.}
elements.sent-2411-135: From this ranking, we selected the top 4K images as our final benchmark set, with an average of seven tags per image.
elements.sent-2411-136: In our benchmark, we assume the speaker is outside the scene and talks to a person inside the image.
elements.sent-2411-137: However, identifying the addressee is not always straightforward, since each image contains at least two people.
elements.sent-2411-138: Although grounding the person specified by utterance can make our task even more complex, it is not a focal aspect of our evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-139: To relieve burden coming from ambiguous addressee, we used provided bounding boxes from VCR and assigned numbers to the center of each bounding box.
elements.sent-2411-140: After applying indicators, we got five people per image in average.
elements.sent-2411-141: Although the direct prompt is not used in our task problem itself, it serves an important foundation for crafting the indirect prompt, as they hold the same underlying intention.
elements.sent-2411-142: We generate the direct prompt conditioned on the input image $I$ and a task prompt $T$ that includes the definition of relevance and solvability discussed in Section \ref{sec:metdef to align the output with the definition of directness.}
elements.sent-2411-143: While generating direct prompt, we simultaneously ask the model to output the solution triplet in the following format: (subject, action, object).
elements.sent-2411-144: Since direct prompts explicitly mention their intentions in the utterance itself, extracting each component from the solution triplet is straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-145: Importantly, the "object" from the solution triplet is restricted among extracted physical objects to meet the solvability discussed in Section \ref{sec:directness .}
elements.sent-2411-146: Previously, in Section \ref{sec:metdef , we explain that the direct and indirect prompts share the same underlying intention, which allows us to use the direct prompt as a basis for generating its indirect counterpart.}
elements.sent-2411-147: The indirect prompt, however, involves complex requirements that must be met, often resulting in generated outputs that are not consistently satisfactory in the human perspective.
elements.sent-2411-148: To address this issue, our approach leverages off-the-shelf VLMs to generate indirect prompts through a two-stage process: proposal and verification.
elements.sent-2411-149: In the initial step, the VLM is prompted to produce three distinct candidate options following the criteria explained in Section \ref{sec:indirectness .}
elements.sent-2411-150: We then prompt the model to identify the candidate that best obscures the direct prompt's intention while avoiding explicit references to actions or functionalities inherently tied to the objects in the direct prompt.
elements.sent-2411-151: In the subsequent process, we calculate the BERT F1-score \cite{bert-score between the direct prompt and each of the three candidates.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Based on these scores, the options are ranked on a scale from one to three, with three assigned to the candidate demonstrating the lowest BERT F1-score \citep{bert-score .}
elements.sent-2411-153: We then calculate the total score across all prompts to evaluate the alignment between the score ranks and model selection.
elements.sent-2411-154: The computation turns out to be that the average score per sample is 2.19 out of 3.
elements.sent-2411-155: This result demonstrates that the model consistently selects the options that diverge contextually from the direct prompt.
elements.sent-2411-156: This model-driven selection with verification ensures that the chosen prompt genuinely disguises the intention in a meaningful way.
elements.sent-2411-157: While creating the correct choice can be easily derived from a direct prompts, generating high-quality counterfactual choices for multiple-choice questions is both important and challenging.
elements.sent-2411-158: To provide more realistic and plausible options for humans, and to trace the reasoning process from the model's choice, we developed interpretable counterfactual choices.
elements.sent-2411-159: Before exploring each, we first identified the capabilities required for multimodal intent disambiguation as image inference and text understanding.
elements.sent-2411-160: For cases where one of these tasks is not performed well, we designed three plausible interpretations alongside the correct choice.
elements.sent-2411-161: To create each option, we used well-curated prompts with GPT-4o, which demonstrates state-of-the-art performance.
elements.sent-2411-162: Additionally, we restricted all answers to no more than 15 words to ensure they have similar lengths, all starting with "The speaker wants person $N$ to." Our counterfactual choices allow for detailed analysis of specific weaknesses when testing the multimodal intent disambiguation ability across various models and question types, as we have shown in Section \ref{sec:results .}
elements.sent-2411-163: The first counterfactual choice is an interpretation that could arise when the model largely ignores the image.
elements.sent-2411-164: This process is conducted in two steps.
elements.sent-2411-165: In the first step, a fake caption is generated by assuming an imaginary scene.
elements.sent-2411-166: In the second step, the generated fake caption is combined with the speaker's indirect statement to derive the most likely interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-167: However, if the fake scene imagined in the first step does not overlap at all with the actual image, the resulting choice might be too apart and easy.
elements.sent-2411-168: To address this, physical objects present in the actual image are provided as prior knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-169: Nonetheless, if any entity provided as a prior is part of the solution triplet, the fake scene might end up being too similar to the real image.
elements.sent-2411-170: To balance between getting too similar or dissimilar, only two entities unrelated to the solution are selected as priors.
elements.sent-2411-171: Once a suitably distracting fake scene caption is generated, it is now able to be combined with the indirect prompt in the second step for generating a plausible interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-172: The subsequent choice corresponds to an interpretation generated when the model fails to reason about the implicit intent of the sentence and instead relies on surface-level understanding.
elements.sent-2411-173: We enforce the model to focus only on the literal wording, without considering any implied or deeper meaning of the indirect sentence.
elements.sent-2411-174: To enhance quality, three curated example cases carefully curated by humans were provided at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-175: The last choice arises when the model interprets the text correctly but fails to adequately consider the details of the image, resulting in a plausible yet incorrect choice.
elements.sent-2411-176: This resembles the correct answer in structure or is a simple paraphrase of it but replaces the solution entity with one that does not exist in the image.
elements.sent-2411-177: To prevent the model from generating implausible or irrelevant entities, we constrain the substituted object to one that, while absent from the image, is highly expected to be present in the scene and could reasonably replace the solution entity.

elements.sent-2411-233: While crafting the VAGUE benchmark and evaluating it on various models, we encountered several limitations.

elements.sent-2411-244: Our research introduces a novel benchmark VAGUE: Visual Contexts ClArify ambiGUous Expressions, designed to advance the capabilities of models to comprehend nuanced, indirect communication in complex multimodal contexts.
elements.sent-2411-245: By addressing the challenges of interpreting hidden intentions in human interactions, our benchmark expands the scope of reasoning beyond explicit textual inputs, setting a new standard for evaluating indirect and context-dependent understanding.
elements.sent-2411-246: The model-based pipeline offers scalability and efficiency in generating prompt-solution pairs, enabling more robust analysis of model performance.
elements.sent-2411-247: Extensive evaluations highlight the limitations of current vision-language models in handling ambiguous communication, emphasizing the need for further advancements.
elements.sent-2411-248: Our work opens up promising avenues for research in developing systems capable of deeper reasoning and more natural, human-like interactions.
elements.sent-2411-249: Human communication often relies on visual cues to resolve ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-250: While humans can intuitively integrate these cues, AI systems often find it challenging to engage in sophisticated multimodal reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-251: We introduce VAGUE, a benchmark evaluating multimodal AI systems' ability to integrate visual context for intent disambiguation.
elements.sent-2411-252: VAGUE consists of 1.6K ambiguous textual expressions, each paired with an image and multiple-choice interpretations, where the correct answer is only apparent with visual context.
elements.sent-2411-253: The dataset spans both staged, complex (Visual Commonsense Reasoning) and natural, personal (Ego4D) scenes, ensuring diversity.
elements.sent-2411-254: Our experiments reveal that existing multimodal AI models struggle to infer the speaker's true intent.
elements.sent-2411-255: While performance consistently improves from the introduction of more visual cues, the overall accuracy remains far below human performance, highlighting a critical gap in multimodal reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-256: Analysis of failure cases demonstrates that current models fail to distinguish true intent from superficial correlations in the visual scene, indicating that they perceive images but do not effectively reason with them.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/motiv.pdf} \caption{ A motivating example demonstrating the importance of visual context in understanding intention. Without a predetermined context, a single expression can convey multiple different intentions. The textual expression and Context 1 are from our dataset, while Context 2 is generated using DALL·E 3 \cite{dalle} to help understanding. } \label{fig:motiv}
elements.sent-2411-257: Human communication is inherently contextual; for example, exclaiming “Hey, this is a disaster!” upon seeing a cluttered room conveys frustration or exaggeration rather than referring to an actual catastrophe.
elements.sent-2411-258: Without surrounding cues, textual dialogues can be ambiguous, making it difficult for models to accurately capture intent and nuance.
elements.sent-2411-259: We consider the case of visual contextual cues.
elements.sent-2411-260: Consider~\cref{fig:motiv , which depicts a speaker making a remark in a certain situation.}
elements.sent-2411-261: Without specifying contexts introduced from visual cues, the speaker's intention can vary, thus remaining ambiguous.
elements.sent-2411-262: This implies that the visual contexts play important roles in communication, raising the question: can AI systems integrate visual cues with ambiguous dialogue to infer the speaker's intent? We introduce Visual Contexts ClArify ambiGUous Expressions (VAGUE), a benchmark consisting of 1.6K ambiguous textual expressions, each paired with a single image.
elements.sent-2411-263: VAGUE aims to model diverse and natural human-to-human interactions by setting each image as the speaker’s viewpoint, where the speaker implicitly requests a certain action from a person within their field of view.
elements.sent-2411-264: We define the problem addressed through this setup as Multimodal Intention Disambiguation (MID), which involves reasoning about the most plausible request conditioned on visual context.
elements.sent-2411-265: Each sample in VAGUE is annotated with four multiple-choice candidates, ensuring clarity and preventing multiple valid answers caused by paraphrasing or hierarchical inclusion of meaning.
elements.sent-2411-266: The dataset is meticulously curated to ensure visual dependency; the ground-truth candidate is only preferable when considering the visual context.
elements.sent-2411-267: VAGUE includes visual scenes from both artificial sources (Visual Commonsense Reasoning~\cite{vcr ) and real-world scenarios (Ego4D~\cite{ego4d ), capturing a broad spectrum of scene complexity and naturalness.}}
elements.sent-2411-268: The textual expressions in VAGUE are initially generated by GPT-4o \cite{openai2023gpt4 following instructions, then reviewed through extensive human rating and filtering to ensure naturalness and alignment with the corresponding images.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Experiments on VAGUE demonstrate that existing multimodal AI models struggle to infer a speaker's true intent in a multimodal setting.
elements.sent-2411-270: First, although models can leverage visual context—as seen by a performance progression from text-only Language Models (LMs) to pipelined Socratic Models (SMs)~\cite{zengsocratic and ultimately to end-to-end Visual Language Models (VLMs)—their overall accuracy remains significantly lower than that of humans, indicating a failure to capture the true intent.}
elements.sent-2411-271: A closer analysis of failure cases reveals that the primary source of error is the models' inability to distinguish the true intent from a superficial understanding of the visual context.
elements.sent-2411-272: In other words, even though these multimodal systems can perceive the image content, they cannot effectively use this information to reason about the speaker's true intent.
elements.sent-2411-273: In conclusion, we introduce a benchmark that exposes the limitations of current models in integrating visual cues with intent comprehension and identifies their primary failure mode.
elements.sent-2411-274: We anticipate that VAGUE will serve as a testing ground for the development of future multimodal conversational or embodied agents—systems that combine robust visual perception with nuanced conversational reasoning to effectively respond to user requests in complex scenes.
elements.sent-2411-276: VAGUE: a novel benchmark for evaluating multimodal intention disambiguation.
elements.sent-2411-277: Validated through extensive human filtering, VAGUE is designed for robust quantitative assessment by ensuring both the ambiguity of queries and the visual (in)dependency to answer candidates.
elements.sent-2411-278: Carefully curated 1,677 scene images sourced from VCR~\cite{vcr and Ego4D~\cite{ego4d , capturing a wide range of scene complexity, diversity, and naturalness to ensure VAGUE’s generalizability across various contexts.}}
elements.sent-2411-279: Experimental results highlighting a critical challenge in multimodal intention disambiguation: while existing models can perceive visual cues, they fail to effectively integrate this information into reasoning to deduce the speaker's true intent.
elements.sent-2411-280: Theory of Mind (ToM) refers to the ability to infer and reason about the intentions of others based on available information \cite{premack1978does , where recent language models still struggle with relevant tasks \cite{cuskley2024limitations highlighting the need for dedicated research in this area.}}

elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/task.pdf} \caption{ Description of the Multimodal Intention Disambiguation (MID) task in a Multiple-Choice Question format: Given an input image $(I)$ and an indirect expression $(p_i)$, the goal is to infer the speaker's hidden intent $(T)$ and select the most likely answer. } \label{fig:task}
elements.sent-2411-305: In this section, we outline the structure and rationale behind the format of our primary task, which we term Multimodal Intention Disambiguation (MID).
elements.sent-2411-306: Then, we further specify the necessary components that form the basis of the task.
elements.sent-2411-307: Each MID problem comprises an input image \(I\), a direct text expression \(p_{d}\), and an indirect text expression \(p_{i}\).
elements.sent-2411-308: Here, the direct expression \(p_{d}\) clearly shows the underlying intention of the corresponding \(p_{i}\) and serves as an essential intermediate step of generating \(p_{i}\).
elements.sent-2411-309: To clarify our problem, we assume that all reasoning is confined to the depicted scene and that each expression is spoken by a human who intends for the listener to take a particular action based on the situation.
elements.sent-2411-310: The ultimate objective of the task is to interpret the hidden intention \(T\) effectively by leveraging the contextual cues within the image.
elements.sent-2411-311: To evaluate how well models capture such intentions, we adopt a multiple-choice (MCQ) format as the primary setup, as shown in \cref{fig:task .}
elements.sent-2411-312: This decision reflects the fact that certain prompts can lead to multiple plausible outcomes, driven by hierarchical relations (e.g., pick up the chips - snack - food) or by the inherent uncertainty of what action best satisfies the speaker’s goal (e.g., an indirect prompt complaining about darkness could be addressed by either turning on a light or opening curtains).
elements.sent-2411-313: Exploring all possible valid interpretations is labor-intensive and often infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-314: Consequently, each MID instance is presented as four distinct options, one correct and three intentionally designed to be incorrect for different reasons (see \cref{subsubsec:mcq ), challenging models in both linguistic and visual reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-315: Formally, let \(C\) be the set of all multiple-choice options \(c_n\).
elements.sent-2411-316: Given an image \(I\) and an indirect prompt \(p_{i}\), the task is to select the most valid interpretation of \(p_{i}\) from the predefined options, conditioned on the visual context in \(I\).
elements.sent-2411-317: We define this task as follows: \begin{equation}T(I, p_{i}) := \operatorname{argmax}_{c_n \in C} \, \Pr(c_n \mid I, p_{i}). \label{eq:intention}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-318: By the design of our task, curating effective input prompts \(p_d\) and \(p_i\) is crucial for ensuring accurate interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-319: What makes a good prompt, though? In this section, we define and explain the criteria that both direct and indirect expressions must satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-320: For more details on good and bad examples for each criterion, please refer to our \cref{app:textdef_example .}
elements.sent-2411-321: The direct prompt is an utterance from the speaker that explicitly conveys its intended meaning without ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-322: However, it is equally important that this intention aligns with the visual context of the scene.
elements.sent-2411-323: For example, a direct prompt \( p_d \) such as “Hey person1, I want you to stop the fireworks” clearly expresses its intended action.
elements.sent-2411-324: However, if the corresponding image, as shown in \cref{fig:task , contains no elements related to fireworks, the prompt is misaligned with the scene.}
elements.sent-2411-325: Thus, a direct prompt must not only reveal its intention but also maintain relevance to the image.
elements.sent-2411-326: In the context of our task, relevance is determined by whether a human can reasonably establish a connection between the prompt and the depicted scene.
elements.sent-2411-327: Relevance alone does not guarantee that a prompt is useful.
elements.sent-2411-328: As outlined in \cref{sec:prob , the prompt \( p_d \) must explicitly request an action that the listener can reasonably perform.}
elements.sent-2411-329: This introduces solvability as an additional criterion, which requires that the prompt present a clear and actionable problem.

Based on human-defined criteria and instructions, GPT-4o \cite{openai2023gpt4} generates initial data, which are then rated and filtered by humans to ensure quality. elements.sent-2411-408: Our first objective is to assess how effectively MLLMs leverage visual cues to resolve ambiguity in utterances.
elements.sent-2411-409: To this end, we systematically control the level of detail in the visual cues provided to the models and measure their accuracy in inferring the speaker’s true intent.
elements.sent-2411-410: Performance is evaluated in both multiple-choice and free-form settings.
elements.sent-2411-411: For clarity, we primarily report multiple-choice accuracy, deferring free-form results to~\cref{app:fullres .}
elements.sent-2411-412: We consider three levels of visual cues:.
elements.sent-2411-413: Language Models (LMs) receive no visual input, requiring models to rely on superficial textual priors such as common-sense knowledge of sarcasm or humor to determine intent.
elements.sent-2411-414: Socratic Models (SMs)~\cite{zengsocratic use text-only LMs but incorporate short image captions (up to two or three sentences) as additional input.}
elements.sent-2411-415: This setup provides minimal visual context, which may be insufficient for accurately inferring intent.
elements.sent-2411-416: Each SM model generated its own image captions and used them in subsequent processing.
elements.sent-2411-417: Visual Language Models (VLMs) receive the raw image input, enabling a more direct interpretation of visual cues.
elements.sent-2411-418: The results in~\cref{tab:main indicate that MLLMs can leverage visual cues, albeit to a limited extent, since SMs and VLMs consistently outperform LMs across all evaluated models.}
elements.sent-2411-419: Additionally, more detailed visual input generally improves performance, with VLMs surpassing SMs in most cases, except in the case of proprietary models (GPT-4o and Gemini 1.5 Pro).
elements.sent-2411-420: This exception is further analyzed in~\cref{subsec:exp_failure .}
elements.sent-2411-421: Both the VCR and Ego4D subsets exhibit similar performance trends, demonstrating the generalizability of our findings across both staged and real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-422: Finally, the consistently low performance of LMs further reinforces the validity of our dataset as a multimodal benchmark.

elements.sent-2411-426: Conversely, the Superficial Understanding (SU) candidate evaluates the model’s reasoning ability, testing whether it can go beyond surface-level perception to infer intent.

elements.sent-2411-451: We present VAGUE (Visual Contexts ClArify ambiGUous Expressions), a new benchmark aimed at assessing models' ability to interpret nuanced communication in complex multimodal scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-452: Our results show that models benefit from visual information when inferring the underlying intention of indirect expressions, as evidenced by their improved performance with increasing levels of visual cues.
elements.sent-2411-453: However, a significant disparity persists between machine capabilities and human-level understanding.
elements.sent-2411-454: To gain deeper insights into model inaccuracies, we design multiple-choice questions that explicitly address failure points, enabling a systematic and quantitative evaluation of the reasons behind performance.
elements.sent-2411-455: The primary challenge identified is the tendency of multimodal models to inadequately integrate visual cues, relying instead on the literal interpretation of textual information.
elements.sent-2411-456: This shortcoming highlights the need for further research, and we anticipate that VAGUE will open up promising avenues for developing systems capable of deeper multimodal reasoning to enhance AI's ability to engage in human-like interactions.
elements.sent-2411-457: First, there are cultural and linguistic limitations.
elements.sent-2411-458: We incorporate sarcastic, humorous, and idiomatic implicatures in generating indirect expressions.
elements.sent-2411-459: However, since the initial data drafts are created by a model (GPT-4o \cite{openai2023gpt4 ), they may reflect cultural biases present in its training data.}
elements.sent-2411-460: To prevent any potential ethical issues, all human annotators are instructed to remove any content considered problematic or discriminatory during the rating and filtering process.
elements.sent-2411-461: Also, all textual expressions in VAGUE are limited to English.
elements.sent-2411-462: Therefore, we encourage future exploration of indirect expressions across diverse languages and cultures.
elements.sent-2411-463: The second limitation pertains to the dependency of certain meta-information on the quality of the parent dataset \cite{vcr and the performance of the models utilized during the dataset generation process.}
elements.sent-2411-464: We observe that bounding box annotations from YOLOv11 \cite{yolo are occasionally duplicated, leading to a reported number of people higher than actually present.}
elements.sent-2411-465: Likewise, the tagging model RAM \cite{ram sometimes misidentified objects.}
elements.sent-2411-466: To address this issue, we remove any corrupted instances that could undermine the integrity of the task during the human rating and filtering process.

elements.sent-2411-490: To facilitate high-quality annotations efficiently, we use Label Studio (\url{https://labelstud.io/ ).}
elements.sent-2411-491: To ensure broad coverage of existing multimodal language models (MLLMs), we evaluate eight open-source models with varying parameter sizes alongside two closed-source models.
elements.sent-2411-492: For the open-source models, we use Phi3.5-Vision-Instruct (4B) \cite{phi , optimized for concise instruction-following tasks, providing robust text-image alignment in low-parameter settings.}
elements.sent-2411-493: LLaVA Onevision (7B) \cite{onevision focuses on high-resolution image interpretation, enhancing multimodal dialogue through refined attention mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-494: Qwen2.5-VL-Instruct (7B) \cite{qwen uses advanced vision-language pretraining to handle diverse image-based queries and textual instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-495: InternVL-2.5-MPO (8B, 26B) \cite{internvl is designed for multi-purpose optimizations, supporting enhanced multimodal reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-496: Idefics2 (8B) \cite{idefics adopts a compact architecture for efficient training, emphasizing domain-specific image understanding and textual generation.}
elements.sent-2411-497: LLaVA NeXT Vicuna (13B) \cite{li2024llavanextinterleavetacklingmultiimagevideo employs an improved vision encoder and refined instruction tuning for enhanced commonsense reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-498: Ovis2 (16B) \cite{ovis excels in image captioning and inference, driven by robust textual grounding and visual alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-499: Among proprietary models, GPT-4o \cite{openai2023gpt4 demonstrates advanced language comprehension paired with visual perception for nuanced multimodal interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-500: Gemini 1.5 Pro \cite{gemini15 integrates high-resolution vision processing with a powerful language model, delivering refined instruction-following and cross-domain reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-501: The BLEU score is a metric for assessing the quality of machine-generated text by comparing it to a reference.
elements.sent-2411-502: It measures n-gram precision, checking how many n-grams from the generated text appear in the reference.
elements.sent-2411-503: However, when evaluating free-form generated text with BLEU, the score drops from 2-grams onward due to the high variability in phrasing.
elements.sent-2411-504: To obtain a more meaningful measure, we use 1-gram BLEU, which captures individual word overlap and provides a reasonable approximation of text similarity.
elements.sent-2411-505: BERT-F1 is a semantic similarity metric that utilizes contextual embeddings from the BERT model.
elements.sent-2411-506: Instead of relying on exact word matches, it calculates an F1-score based on token similarity in an embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-507: This allows it to capture paraphrasing and synonymy, making it more effective at evaluating meaning rather than just surface-level similarity.
elements.sent-2411-508: Traditional text similarity metrics such as BLEU \cite{papineni-etal-2002-bleu and BERT-F1 \cite{bert-score are widely used for evaluating language models.}}
elements.sent-2411-509: However, they are often not well-suited for assessing intent similarity in multimodal intent disambiguation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-510: BLEU computes n-gram overlap between sentences, treating all tokens equally, which makes it ineffective in capturing subtle intent differences.
elements.sent-2411-511: Similarly, BERT-F1, which uses contextual embeddings, struggles with antonymy, as opposing words often appear in similar contexts.
elements.sent-2411-512: For example, ``open the window'' and ``close the window'' have completely opposite intents, yet BERT-F1 assigns them a high similarity score of 0.939 due to shared structure and overlapping words.
elements.sent-2411-513: To address this, we adopt a multiple-choice question (MCQ) format as our main setting, which directly evaluates whether a model selects the correct intent rather than relying on approximate similarity scores.
elements.sent-2411-514: The MCQ structure explicitly includes plausible but incorrect distractors, ensuring that models must resolve ambiguity by integrating multimodal cues rather than relying on lexical overlap alone.
elements.sent-2411-515: This structured approach enables a more robust and intent-aware evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-516: It shows that the underlying intention behind the indirect expression in the given image is well preserved, and the generated responses across different settings are highly similar.
elements.sent-2411-517: For human evaluation, we use a subset of 400 high-quality items from VAGUE.
elements.sent-2411-518: These items are carefully selected to ensure that the intended correct answer aligns well with the image and that the indirect expression remains sufficiently ambiguous.
elements.sent-2411-519: Low-quality samples have already been filtered out, resulting in direct expressions with scores of 4 or 5 and indirect expressions with scores ranging from 3 to 5.
elements.sent-2411-520: Due to this filtering, the average scores of direct and indirect expressions are often tied.
elements.sent-2411-521: In such cases, we randomly select items to form the final 400-item subset.
elements.sent-2411-522: Our human evaluator is a student researcher with expertise in human cognition across modalities and language models.
elements.sent-2411-523: The evaluator, a Korean fluent in English at a native level, annotated all 400 items independently.
